title: Pastor and Gubernatorial Candidate Dan Fisher Responds to Oklahoma Baptist Leaders Opposing SB13
author: Free The States
orientation: Southern Baptist pastor and former Oklahoma legislator Dan Fisher publicly rebukes the Baptist General Convention of Oklahoma for opposing SB13, arguing that incremental pro-life regulation has failed and that Christians are biblically obligated to defy unjust civil authority and demand the immediate abolition of abortion.
audiences: Southern Baptist pastor weighing whether to break with denominational leadership
Pro-life activist re-evaluating the incremental strategy
Christian legislator wrestling with judicial supremacy and Romans 13
Skeptic asking whether civil disobedience is ever justified
Oklahoma voter trying to understand the SB13 controversy

questions.question: Why is Dan Fisher publicly opposing the leadership of the Baptist General Convention of Oklahoma?
questions.audience: Southern Baptist pastor weighing whether to break with denominational leadership
questions.answer: Fisher believes the BGCO is siding with a forty-five-year incrementalist strategy that merely regulates abortion while waiting for Supreme Court permission, and he feels duty-bound to speak up for those calling for immediate abolition before more babies are killed.
questions.quote: The leaders of the Baptist General Convention of Oklahoma and other groups are standing against preachers and leaders like me who are calling and working for the total and immediate abolition of abortion – as opposed to the incremental approach that has been employed by the Pro-Life movement in general, and the BGCO in particular, for the past forty-five years.

questions.question: What credibility does Fisher bring to this critique of pro-life politics?
questions.audience: Oklahoma voter trying to understand the SB13 controversy
questions.answer: He is a forty-three-year Southern Baptist pastor, served two terms in the Oklahoma Legislature, and ran for governor on a state-sovereignty and abolition platform, finishing fourth in a ten-candidate Republican primary.
questions.quote: In addition, I served two terms in the Oklahoma Legislature and ran a strong campaign for governor on a platform of state sovereignty and abolition of abortion (coming in fourth in a field of ten Republican candidates).

questions.question: What did Fisher learn about Republican politicians during his time in the legislature?
questions.audience: Pro-life activist re-evaluating the incremental strategy
questions.answer: From inside the Capitol he saw that most self-described pro-life Republicans treat abortion as a political football and refuse to take the steps actually required to end it, hiding behind deference to the federal courts.
questions.quote: There, “from the inside,” I learned firsthand the way abortion is used as a football in a political pro-life game played by “conservative, pro-life” politicians. I learned that while most Republican politicians *say* they want to end abortion, they are completely unwilling to *do what it takes* to end it.

questions.question: How does Fisher reconcile civil disobedience with biblical commands to submit to authority?
questions.audience: Christian legislator wrestling with judicial supremacy and Romans 13
questions.answer: He affirms Romans 13 and a healthy respect for office, but insists that submission has limits and that God never requires Christians to give unlimited obedience to evil rulers or evil laws.
questions.quote: I have consistently preached from biblical passages like Romans 13 that Christians should submit to and honor those in authority.  But, living in a representative republic as I do, I believe submission to authority has its limits and that God does not require *unlimited submission* to evil leaders or laws.

questions.question: What historical Christian precedent does Fisher invoke for resisting tyrannical government?
questions.audience: Skeptic asking whether civil disobedience is ever justified
questions.answer: He points to the patriot pastors of the American War of Independence and quotes Rev. Samuel West's 1776 Massachusetts election sermon, which taught that obedience to magistrates cannot be unlimited because rulers exist for the good of the people.
questions.quote: Having done a good deal of research about the “patriot pastors” of our War of Independence (godly preachers who led the men of their churches and communities to fight against tyranny in the eighteenth century), I have come to believe there are times when faithful, law-abiding people must reluctantly stand against their own government.

questions.question: According to Samuel West, when does a magistrate forfeit his authority?
questions.audience: Christian legislator wrestling with judicial supremacy and Romans 13
questions.answer: When the ruler punishes the good and rewards the evil, he becomes a tyrant and a minister of Satan, losing his right to be obeyed and instead deserving to be opposed.
questions.quote: Whenever, then, the ruler encourages them that do evil, and is a terror to those that do well, i.e., as soon as he becomes a tyrant, he forfeits his authority to govern, and becomes the minister of Satan, and, as such, ought to be opposed.

questions.question: Which biblical figures does Fisher cite as models of righteous defiance?
questions.audience: Southern Baptist pastor weighing whether to break with denominational leadership
questions.answer: He lists the Hebrew midwives, Moses, Esther, Shadrach Meshach and Abed-Nego, Daniel, Jesus, and the apostles as scriptural heroes who refused to comply with ungodly rulers.
questions.quote: - The Hebrew midwives defying the command of Pharaoh by saving the Jewish baby boys – including Moses’s parents saving him (Ex 1:15-21, 2:1-10)

questions.question: What modern figures does Fisher use to argue that defying unjust law is honored, not condemned?
questions.audience: Skeptic asking whether civil disobedience is ever justified
questions.answer: He points to nineteenth-century slavery abolitionists, Corrie Ten Boom, Oskar Schindler, and Dietrich Bonhoeffer hiding Jews from the Nazis, and Martin Luther King Jr. in the civil rights movement.
questions.quote: We praise the abolitionists of the 1800s who saw slavery as unconscionable and defied the law to free their neighbors trapped in slavery.  We rightly honor people like Corrie Ten Boom and her family, Oskar Shindler, and Dietrich Bonheoffer for defying the law to save Jews and other political dissidents from the evil clutches of the Nazis in the 1930s and 40s.

questions.question: How does Fisher answer the argument that we must obey the Supreme Court because its rulings are constitutional?
questions.audience: Christian legislator wrestling with judicial supremacy and Romans 13
questions.answer: He lists Dred Scott, forced sterilization, Japanese internment, and same-sex marriage rulings as proof that the Court routinely declares immoral things constitutional, so its declaration on abortion deserves no submission either.
questions.quote: Should Americans have bowed to the Court’s *ruling* in 1857 whey they declared that blacks were *constitutionally* mere property?  Are we compelled to bow to the Court’s 1927 decree that it is *constitutional* to sterilize American citizens against their will?

questions.question: How does Fisher evaluate the track record of the pro-life movement?
questions.audience: Pro-life activist re-evaluating the incremental strategy
questions.answer: Despite good intentions, he sees the movement as a failure, with sixty-two million dead babies nationally and fifteen to twenty killed every business day in Oklahoma.
questions.quote: Without diving deeply into a debate about the merits and motivations of those in the pro-life movement, let me simply say that the evidence is overwhelming, at least to me, that pro-life efforts, though maybe well intentioned, have, for the most part, failed.  I would hardly reckon sixty-two million dead babies (and counting) a success.

questions.question: Does Fisher dismiss the idea that incremental laws save any babies at all?
questions.audience: Pro-life activist re-evaluating the incremental strategy
questions.answer: No, he concedes pro-life efforts have saved some babies, but insists that saving some can never be the goal because Christian faith demands an immediate end to all abortion.
questions.quote: Have pro-life efforts saved babies?  Of course they have.  But is saving *some* babies by slow, *incremental regulations* the goal?  No!  The goal must be the *immediate* and *complete* end of abortion!  Christian faith demands it!

questions.question: What analogy does Fisher use to expose the moral failure of partial measures?
questions.audience: Skeptic asking whether civil disobedience is ever justified
questions.answer: He says no one would call for some slaves to be freed or some Nazi camps to be closed, and if we were the ones being killed we would demand immediate justice rather than a phased solution.
questions.quote: Knowing what we now know, we would never call for *some* slaves to be freed or for *some* Nazi concentration camps to be closed.  We would call for the immediate end to both atrocities.  I can assure you, if it were you or I who were bound in slavery, imprisoned in a concentration camp, or the target of some abortionist’s suction device or scissors, we would not be calling for an incremental solution.

questions.question: What direct question does Fisher leave for BGCO leaders?
questions.audience: Southern Baptist pastor weighing whether to break with denominational leadership
questions.answer: He asks whether their measured response is really expected to fix abortion or whether it is just a way to ease a guilty conscience while the killing continues.
questions.quote: So, why are the leaders of the BGCO calling for slow, measured responses to the wickedness of abortion while thousands/millions continue to be murdered?  Do we really think more regulations will fix the problem – or are we simply attempting to soothe a guilty conscience?

related.title: Oklahoma Baptists Pass Resolution Demanding an Immediate "End" to Abortion
related.reason: Direct follow-up on the BGCO posture toward abolition that Fisher is rebuking here.

related.title: Why "No Bill But SB13"?
related.reason: Explains the SB13 abolition bill that Fisher is defending against Baptist leadership.

related.title: Anti-SB13 Campaigners Aren't Ignorant. They're Trying to Deceive You.
related.reason: Names and counters the very opposition coalition Fisher is responding to.

related.title: Five Reasons Abolitionists Oppose Incremental Pro-Life Bills
related.reason: Systematizes the anti-incrementalist case Fisher makes anecdotally from his legislative experience.

related.title: Prominent Southern Baptist Voice Calls For Total Abolition
related.reason: Another Southern Baptist breaking with denominational caution to demand immediate abolition, parallel to Fisher's stand.

related.title: The Three Pro-Life Men Keeping Abortion Legal in Oklahoma
related.reason: Concrete Oklahoma evidence for Fisher's claim that pro-life politicians are unwilling to do what it takes to end abortion.

related.title: Abolitionist, Not Pro-Life
related.reason: Frames the broader abolitionist-vs-pro-life distinction Fisher is drawing in his rebuke.
